analogy: Think of "Freedom" through the chapter's own opening images: Chapter 2 Freedom Human history provides many examples of people and communities which have been dominated, or enslaved, or exploited, by more powerful groups. But it also provides us with inspiring examples of heroic struggles against such domination. What is this freedom for which people... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Freedom is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that chapter 2 Freedom Human history provides many examples of people and communities which have been dominated, or enslaved, or exploited, by more powerful groups.

The text explains that but it also provides us with inspiring examples of heroic struggles against such domination.

The text explains that what is this freedom for which people have been willing to sacrifice and die?

The text explains that in its essence, the struggle for freedom represents the desire of people to be in control of their own lives and destinies and to have the opportunity to express themselves freely through their choices and activities.

The text explains that not just individuals but societies also value their independence and wish to protect their culture and future.

The text explains that however, given the diverse interests and ambitions of people any form of social living requires some rules and regulation.

The text explains that these rules may require some constraints to be imposed on the freedom of individuals but it is recognised that such constraints may also free us from insecurity and provide us with the conditions in which we can develop ourselves.

The text explains that in political theory much of the discussion regarding freedom has therefore focused on trying to evolve principles by which we can distinguish between socially necessary constraints and other restrictions.

The text explains that there has also been debate about possible limitations on freedom which may result from the social and economic structures of a society.

The text explains that in this chapter we will look at some of these debates.

The text explains that after studying this chapter you should be able to: o Understand the importance of freedom for individuals and societies. o Explain the difference between the negative and positive dimensions of freedom. o Explain what is meant by the term ‘harm principle’.

The text explains that freedom FreedomPolitical Theory 18 2.1 THE IDEAL OF FREEDOM Before we set out to answer these questions, let us stop for a moment and consider this.

The text explains that the autobiography of one of the greatest persons of the twentieth century, Nelson Mandela, is titled Long Walk to Freedom.

The text explains that in this book he talks about his personal struggle against the apartheid regime in South Africa, about the resistance of his people to the segregationist policies of the white regime, about the humiliations, hardships and police brutalities suffered by the black people of South Africa.

The text explains that these ranged from being bundled into townships and being denied easy movement about the country, to being denied a free choice of whom to marry.

The text explains that collectively, such measures constituted a body of constraints imposed by the apartheid regime that discriminated between citizens based on their race.

The text explains that gandhiji’s thoughts on non-violence have been a source of inspiration for Aung San Suu Kyi as she remained under house arrest in Myanmar, separated from her children, unable to visit her husband when he was dying of cancer, because she feared that if she left Myanmar to visit him in England she would not be able to return.

The text explains that to understand which social constraints are necessary, discussions on freedom need to look at the core relationship between the individual and the society (or group, community, or state) within which she/he is placed.

The text explains that the Sources of Constraints Restrictions on the freedom of individuals may come from domination and external controls.

The text explains that differences may exist between people regarding their ideas and opinions, they may have conflicting ambitions, they may compete to control scarce resources.

The text explains that however, as freedom is at the core of human society, is so crucial for a dignified human life, it should only be constrained in special circumstances.

The text explains that that is, the person must not be constrained by poverty or unemployment; they must have adequate material resources to pursue their wants and needs.

The text explains that we have now come to realise that freedom embodies our capacity and our ability to make choices.

The text explains that and when we make choices, we have also to accept responsibility for our actions and their consequences.

The text explains that it is for this reason that most advocates of liberty and freedom maintain that children must be placed in the care of parents.

The text explains that our capacity to make the right choices, to assess in a reasoned manner available options, and shoulder the responsibility of our actions, have to be built through education and cultivation of judgement just as much as it needs to be nurtured by limiting the authority of the state and the society.

The text explains that is there a relationship between freedom for the individual and freedom for the nation?

The text explains that what is the difference between the negative and positive conception of liberty?

The text explains that are constraints of any kind necessary for enjoying freedom?

The text explains that what is the role of the state in upholding freedom of its citizens?

The text explains that what in your view would be a reasonable restriction on this freedom?

The text explains that credit: Images on opening page: http://www.africawithin.com (Nelson Mandela) and http://www.ibiblio.org (Suu Kyi)

Source: NCERT Class 11 Political Theory, Chapter 2: Freedom

prelims_traps: Do not treat 2.1 THE IDEAL OF FREEDOM and 2.2 WHAT IS FREEDOM? as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Freedom; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Freedom through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 2.1 THE IDEAL OF FREEDOM and 2.2 WHAT IS FREEDOM? to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
